Q004.Pathways: Which career directions suit you — e.g. HR, training, operations, design. The app will recommend specific job paths.
Q004.Salary clarity: How much you can expect to earn in different careers, compared to what you earn now.
Q004.Study plan: What courses, certifications, or training you'd need — and how long they take.
Q004.Relocation plan: If you're thinking of moving countries — visa options, job markets, and timelines.
Q004.Entrepreneurship: Starting your own business — freelancing, consulting, or launching a company.
Q004.Advisor coaching: A 1-on-1 session with a human career advisor to discuss your options personally.

Q008.Direct: Short, clear, to the point — no extra words. Just tell me what to do.
Q008.Detailed: Thorough explanations with context and reasoning behind each recommendation.
Q008.Motivational: Encouraging and energising — highlights your strengths and opportunities.
Q008.Minimal: Bullet points only — the essentials with no narrative text.

Q012.GCC legacy carrier: A full-service airline based in the Gulf region — Emirates, Etihad, Qatar Airways, Saudia, Gulf Air, etc.
Q012.Low-cost carrier: A budget airline — flydubai, Air Arabia, Wizz Air, Ryanair, IndiGo, Cebu Pacific, etc.
Q012.Charter: Airlines that operate on-demand or seasonal flights — not scheduled routes.
Q012.Other: Any airline not listed above — regional carriers, cargo airlines, or airlines from other regions.

Q014.Senior: Senior cabin crew member — additional responsibilities beyond standard crew
Q014.Lead: Led a cabin section or team during flights
Q014.Purser-like duties: In charge of the entire cabin — managed crew, handled reports, liaised with flight deck. Even if your title wasn't 'Purser'.

Q015.Trainer support: Helped train new crew members onboard or in the training centre
Q015.Safety rep: Represented your crew on safety matters — reported issues, attended safety meetings
Q015.Service excellence: Part of a team focused on improving passenger service standards
Q015.Onboarding buddy: Assigned to help new joiners settle in during their first weeks/months
Q015.Language speaker: Used as a designated speaker for a specific language onboard
Q015.Documentation: Responsible for reports, paperwork, or record-keeping beyond normal duties

Q024.Handled a medical emergency: e.g. a passenger fainted, had a seizure, or needed oxygen — you took charge of first aid and coordinated with the flight deck
Q024.De-escalated a difficult passenger: e.g. a passenger refused to buckle up, was aggressive to crew, or caused a disturbance — you calmed the situation without escalation
Q024.Led the cabin crew team through a challe: e.g. during a diversion, delay, or staffing issue — you coordinated the team and kept service running
Q024.Delivered exceptional service to a passe: e.g. went above and beyond for a nervous flyer, a family with children, or a VIP — and received genuine thanks
Q024.Managed a safety incident calmly: e.g. turbulence injury, smoke in cabin, emergency landing prep — you followed procedure while keeping passengers calm
Q024.Trained or mentored new crew: e.g. guided a new joiner through their first flights, helped them build confidence, or ran onboard briefings
Q024.Solved an operational problem creatively: e.g. found a workaround for broken equipment, managed catering shortfall, or reorganised seating to solve a problem
Q024.Received recognition or an award: e.g. commendation email, service excellence award, passenger letter, or management recognition

Q025.Severe turbulence or emergency landing: e.g. unexpected clear air turbulence causing injuries, or an emergency diversion that felt genuinely dangerous
Q025.Verbally abusive passenger: e.g. a passenger screaming, using slurs, or personally targeting you — beyond normal difficult behaviour
Q025.Conflict with crew or management: e.g. a senior crew member undermining you, unfair rostering, or being blamed for something that wasn't your fault
Q025.Exhaustion or burnout episode: e.g. back-to-back flights with no recovery, falling asleep on the bus home, or feeling unable to go back to work
Q025.Medical emergency beyond your training: e.g. a passenger needed care you weren't equipped to give — felt helpless or scared
Q025.Unfair treatment by management: e.g. denied promotion without reason, punished for calling in sick, or policy applied inconsistently
Q025.Personal safety felt threatened: e.g. a passenger invaded your space, followed you, or made you feel physically unsafe
Q025.Constant schedule disruption: e.g. roster changes destroying personal plans repeatedly, missed family events, inability to plan life

Q047.Structured: Clear processes, defined roles, predictable routines — like airline operations but on the ground
Q047.Fast-changing: Dynamic priorities, frequent pivots, startup-like energy — suits people who get bored with routine
Q047.Creative: Design, content, visual work, brainstorming — roles where you make things or shape how they look and feel
Q047.Analytical: Data, research, problem-solving, spreadsheets — roles where you figure things out systematically
Q047.Mission-driven: Nonprofits, healthcare, education, sustainability — work where the purpose matters as much as the pay
Q047.Luxury/service: Hotels, private aviation, high-end retail — premium environments similar to business/first class service

Q106.L&D: Learning & Development — corporate training, instructional design, coaching employees
Q106.HR: Human Resources — recruitment, employee relations, people management, talent strategy
Q106.CX: Customer Experience — designing and managing how customers interact with a brand or service
Q106.Operations: Operations & Logistics — scheduling, process management, supply chain, coordination
Q106.Safety: Safety & Compliance — HSE, quality assurance, audit, regulatory roles across industries
Q106.Tech: Technology & Digital — business analysis, UX design, data analytics, digital transformation
Q106.Entrepreneurship: Starting your own business — freelancing, consulting, coaching practice, or service company
Q106.Creative & Design: Graphic design, photography, interior design, art direction, visual merchandising
Q106.Media & Communications: PR, corporate comms, social media management, content strategy, event coordination
Q106.Healthcare: Non-clinical healthcare roles — patient experience, clinic coordination, healthcare admin
Q106.Education & Coaching: Teaching, academic coordination, life/career coaching, corporate facilitation
Q106.Hospitality: Hotels, cruise lines, luxury retail, VIP services — premium service management
Q106.Sales: B2B/B2C sales, account management, business development — commission-based earning potential

Q042.Low: You mainly use your phone — email and basic apps. Spreadsheets and software feel unfamiliar.
Q042.Medium: Comfortable with email, basic Excel, and common apps. Can learn new tools with some guidance.
Q042.High: Confident with multiple software tools, can self-teach new tech, and enjoy digital problem-solving.

Q054.Individual contributor: You want to do great work without managing people — specialist, analyst, designer, or expert roles
Q054.Manager: You want to lead a team of 3-15 people, set direction, and develop others
Q054.Senior leader: You aspire to director/VP level — shaping strategy, managing managers, influencing the organisation
Q054.Unsure: You haven't decided yet — that's fine, we'll explore both paths in your results

Q109.Career growth ceiling: You've hit the top of what's available in your current role and want more
Q109.Burnout/wellbeing: The lifestyle, schedule, or emotional demands are taking a toll on your health
Q109.Medical/health: A health condition means flying is no longer viable or safe for you
Q109.Redundancy or contract ending: Your airline is restructuring, or your contract won't be renewed
Q109.Financial: The pay isn't enough for your needs, or the career doesn't offer the growth you want
Q109.Family/lifestyle: You need stability, predictable hours, or to be home for family
Q109.Curiosity: You're not unhappy — just wondering what else is out there

Q057.Work-life balance: Predictable hours, no constant on-call, ability to plan your personal life
Q057.Minimum salary met: Your next role must meet a specific income floor — no negotiation
Q057.Location stability: You need to stay in your current city/country — relocation isn't an option
Q057.Health benefits: Medical insurance for you and/or family is essential — not optional
Q057.Remote or hybrid work: You need flexibility to work from home at least part of the week
Q057.Career growth path: Clear promotion track — you won't accept a dead-end role
Q057.Job security: Permanent contract preferred — no short-term gigs or unstable startups
Q057.Family-friendly hours: School hours, no weekends, or flexibility for childcare/family needs
Q057.Respectful culture: Zero tolerance for toxic management, bullying, or discrimination
Q057.Learning opportunities: Employer-supported training, courses, or professional development

Q058.Night shifts: Any role requiring regular overnight or early-morning shifts
Q058.Long commute: More than 45-60 minutes each way — not worth the time cost
Q058.Toxic or high-pressure culture: Constant targets, blame culture, aggressive management, or unethical practices
Q058.Sales targets: Being measured on revenue, quotas, or commission — the pressure of selling
Q058.Constant customer complaints: Front-line complaint handling as a primary job function
Q058.Physical labor: Roles requiring heavy lifting, standing all day, or physical strain
Q058.Unpaid overtime: Cultures where long hours are expected but not compensated
Q058.Low pay: Below your minimum acceptable salary — even if the role is interesting
Q058.Micromanagement: Being closely monitored, needing approval for everything, no autonomy
Q058.Working in isolation: Fully solo work with minimal team interaction or collaboration

Q061.Employer-sponsored visa: Your current employer sponsors your right to live and work here — leaving means losing the visa
Q061.Permanent residency: You have PR/indefinite leave — you can work for any employer without sponsorship
Q061.Citizen: You hold citizenship in your country of residence
Q061.Family/spouse visa: Your visa is tied to a family member's employment or residency status
Q061.Self-sponsored visa: You hold your own visa (e.g. freelancer visa, investor visa) independent of an employer
Q061.Student visa: You're on a student visa with limited work rights
Q061.Not sure: You're uncertain about your exact visa status — we'll treat this conservatively

Q070.No: You cannot afford or aren't willing to stop earning — any career change must happen alongside your current job
Q070.Up to 1 month: You could take a short break (e.g. between jobs) but not much more
Q070.Up to 3 months: You have enough runway for a focused study period — bootcamp or intensive course
Q070.6+ months: You could invest in a longer program — diploma, degree, or extended training

Q071.Yes: You're willing to take a step back in seniority to enter a new field — even if it means lower pay initially
Q071.No: You expect to enter at a level matching your aviation experience — mid-level or above
Q071.Maybe: Depends on the role, salary, and growth potential — you'd consider it for the right opportunity

Q048.Onsite: You prefer going to a physical workplace every day — office, clinic, hotel, airport, etc.
Q048.Hybrid: Mix of office and home — typically 2-3 days onsite per week
Q048.Remote: Fully work-from-home or work-from-anywhere — no regular commute

Q043.Excel: Microsoft Excel or Google Sheets — spreadsheets for data, budgets, tracking
Q043.PowerPoint: Microsoft PowerPoint or Google Slides — creating presentations
Q043.LMS: Learning Management System — platforms like Moodle, SAP SuccessFactors, or your airline's training portal
Q043.Scheduling apps: Crew rostering apps, shift planners, or calendar management tools

Q055.Direct: Tell me what's wrong clearly and honestly — I can handle it
Q055.Coaching: Help me figure it out myself through questions and guidance
Q055.Structured: Give me written feedback with specific examples and a plan to improve

Q059.Stability: A secure, predictable job — steady income, low risk of redundancy
Q059.Income growth: Earning more over time — promotions, raises, or commission potential
Q059.Mission: Working for a cause you believe in — healthcare, education, environment, social impact
Q059.Autonomy: Freedom to make your own decisions — minimal supervision, flexible approach
Q059.Prestige: A role or company that carries respect and status
Q059.Learning: Constantly developing new skills — the role keeps you growing
Q059.Family time: Being available for family — school runs, evenings free, weekends off

Q074.Yes: You're open to working in free zones (like DIFC, DMCC in Dubai) or special business areas with different rules
Q074.No: You prefer regular employment outside of special zones
Q074.Not sure: You don't know what free zones or special economic zones are — that's fine

Q083.Expat-heavy: Areas with many foreign residents — international schools, diverse restaurants, English widely spoken
Q083.Mixed: A blend of local and international communities
Q083.Local: Primarily local population — full cultural immersion, local language needed

Q090.Cash: You prefer a higher monthly salary even if benefits like housing or insurance are limited
Q090.Benefits: You'd accept a lower salary if benefits are strong — free housing, insurance, flights, education allowance
Q090.Balanced: A fair mix of both — decent salary with good benefits

Q096.Yes: You're open to freelancing, tutoring, online work, or a side business alongside your main job
Q096.No: You want to focus on one job only
Q096.Maybe: You'd consider it if the opportunity was right

Q099.Self-study: Online courses, books, videos — learning at your own pace, on your own schedule
Q099.Live cohort: Studying with a group — scheduled online or in-person classes with an instructor and classmates
Q099.University: Formal degree program at a university — structured, longer-term, with a recognised qualification

Q102.PMP/PRINCE2: Project Management certifications — for managing projects and teams in any industry
Q102.CIPD: Chartered Institute of Personnel & Development — the main HR/people management qualification (UK & Gulf)
Q102.NEBOSH/IOSH: Health, Safety & Environment certifications — for safety officer and HSE roles
Q102.IELTS/language tests: English language proficiency tests — required for many visas and university admissions
Q102.Data/analytics: Courses in data analysis, Excel, SQL, Power BI — for analytical and reporting roles
Q102.Coaching & mentoring: Certifications for life coaching, career coaching, or executive coaching (e.g. ICF accredited)
Q102.Customer experience (CCXP): Certified Customer Experience Professional — for customer-facing strategy roles
Q102.UX: User Experience design — for designing websites, apps, and digital products
Q102.Digital marketing: Online marketing skills — social media, SEO, content marketing, Google Ads
Q102.Teaching: Qualifications to teach — CELTA, PGCE, or local teaching certificates
Q102.Real estate license: License to work as a property agent or broker in your country
Q102.Safety: General safety certifications beyond NEBOSH — fire safety, food safety, etc.
Q102.HR: Human Resources certifications — SHRM, CIPD, or local equivalents

Q111.Travel: Visiting different places — even if not flying, you want variety in locations
Q111.Team environment: Working closely with a team — camaraderie, shared goals, social connection
Q111.Clear procedures: Step-by-step processes — you like knowing exactly what to do and when
Q111.Helping people: Making a difference in someone's day — service, care, support
Q111.Prestige: A role that carries respect — impressive job title, well-known company
Q111.Variety: Different tasks every day — not the same routine repeatedly
Q111.Physical activity: Being on your feet, moving around — not stuck at a desk all day

Q117.Yes: You're entitled to a lump sum payment when you leave — common in GCC countries (gratuity) or similar severance
Q117.No: You won't receive any payout when you leave your current role
Q117.Not sure: You don't know — check your contract or HR department

Q119.No: This is your first time seriously considering leaving aviation
Q119.Yes — didn't get far: You looked into it but didn't apply or interview — life got in the way or you weren't ready
Q119.Yes — tried and returned: You actually left aviation but came back — for any reason
Q119.Currently in progress: You're actively interviewing, studying, or preparing to leave right now
